problem:
Let k \in \mathbf{N} and let \alpha, \beta \in \mathbf{R} with \alpha \leq \beta. For a = (a_1, \dots, a_k) \in \mathbf{R}^k, define
\[
p(t) = a_1 + a_2 t + \cdots + a_k t^{k-1}.
\]
Determine whether the set
\[
S = \{a \in \mathbf{R}^k \mid p(0) = 1,\ |p(t)| \leq 1 \text{ for all } t \in [\alpha, \beta]\}
\]
is convex.

Let $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$. For a scalar $\alpha$, define $\alpha C = \{\alpha x \mid x \in C\}$, and for scalars $\alpha, \beta$, define $\alpha C + \beta C = \{u+v \mid u \in \alpha C,\ v \in \beta C\}$. Prove that $C$ is convex if and only if
\[
(\alpha + \beta)C = \alpha C + \beta C
\]
for all nonnegative scalars $\alpha, \beta$.

Let $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$, $b \in \mathbf{R}^m$, $c \in \mathbf{R}^n$, $d \in \mathbf{R}$, and let $E \in \mathbf{R}^{p \times m}$, $f \in \mathbf{R}^p$, $g \in \mathbf{R}^m$, $h \in \mathbf{R}$. Define the linear-fractional maps
\[
\phi : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}^m, \qquad \phi(x) = \frac{Ax + b}{c^T x + d},
\]
and
\[
\psi : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}^p, \qquad \psi(y) = \frac{Ey + f}{g^T y + h},
\]
with domains
\[
\mathbf{dom}\,\phi = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\}, \qquad \mathbf{dom}\,\psi = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^m \mid g^T y + h > 0\}.
\]
Let
\[
M_\phi = \begin{bmatrix} A & b \\ c^T & d \end{bmatrix}, \qquad M_\psi = \begin{bmatrix} E & f \\ g^T & h \end{bmatrix},
\]
and define $\Gamma = \psi \circ \phi$ on
\[
\mathbf{dom}\,\Gamma = \{x \in \mathbf{dom}\,\phi \mid \phi(x) \in \mathbf{dom}\,\psi\}.
\]
Show that $\Gamma$ is linear-fractional, and that its associated matrix is
\[
M_\psi M_\phi =
\begin{bmatrix} E & f \\ g^T & h \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} A & b \\ c^T & d \end{bmatrix}.
\]

Consider the exponential cone
\[
K_{\exp} = \{(x,y,z) \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid y > 0,\ ye^{x/y} \le z\}.
\]
Its dual cone is defined by
\[
K_{\exp}^* = \{u \in \mathbf{R}^3 \mid u^T v \ge 0 \text{ for all } v \in K_{\exp}\}.
\]
The closure of $K_{\exp}$ is obtained by adjoining the points
\[
\{(x,y,z) \mid x \le 0,\ y = 0,\ z \ge 0\},
\]
and you may ignore boundary issues and use the fact that a cone and its closure have the same dual. Determine $K_{\exp}^*$.

Let $C, D \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ be closed convex cones. For any cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, define its dual cone by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
Also define
\[
C^* + D^* = \{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}.
\]
Prove that $C \cap D$ is a convex cone and that $C^* + D^*$ is a convex cone.

Let C, D \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n be closed convex cones. For any cone K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n, define its dual cone by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
Show that
\[
(C \cap D)^* \supseteq C^* + D^*,
\]
where
\[
C^* + D^* = \{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}.
\]

Let $C, D \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ be closed convex cones. For any cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, define its dual cone by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\},
\]
and define
\[
C^* + D^* = \{u + v \mid u \in C^*,\ v \in D^*\}.
\]
Assume that for every closed convex cone $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, we have $K^{**} = K$. Prove that
\[
(C \cap D)^* = C^* + D^*.
\]
In particular, first show that
\[
(C \cap D)^* \subseteq C^* + D^* \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*,
\]
then prove
\[
C \cap D \supseteq (C^* + D^*)^*.
\]

Let A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}, and define
\[
V = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid Ax \succeq 0\},
\]
where Ax \succeq 0 means that each component of Ax is nonnegative. Prove that the dual cone of V is
\[
V^* = \{A^T v \mid v \succeq 0\},
\]
where v \in \mathbf{R}^m and v \succeq 0 means that each component of v is nonnegative.

Let C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n, and define its polar set by
\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]
Show that C^\circ is convex.

Let $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$ be a cone, and let its polar set be defined by
\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]
Determine the polar set $C^\circ$.

Let $\|\cdot\|$ be a norm on $\mathbf{R}^n$, and let
\[
B = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \|x\| \leq 1\}
\]
be its unit ball. For any set $C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n$, define its polar set by
\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \leq 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]
Determine the polar set $B^\circ$.

Let \mathbf{1} \in \mathbf{R}^n denote the all-ones vector, and consider the set
\[
C = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \mathbf{1}^T x = 1,\ x \succeq 0\}.
\]
Determine the polar set
\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \le 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]

Let C \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n be a closed convex set containing 0, and define its polar set by
\[
C^\circ = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid y^T x \le 1 \text{ for all } x \in C\}.
\]
Prove that
\[
(C^\circ)^\circ = C.
\]

Let $K = \{0\} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$. The dual cone of $K$ is defined by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
Describe the dual cone $K^*$.

Let $K \subseteq \mathbf{R}^2$ and define its dual cone by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
Describe the dual cone $K^*$ in the case $K = \mathbf{R}^2$.

Let
\[
K = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid |x_1| \le x_2\}.
\]
Define its dual cone by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
Describe the set $K^*$.

Let
\[
K = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid x_1 + x_2 = 0\}.
\]
Define its dual cone by
\[
K^* = \{y \in \mathbf{R}^2 \mid y^T x \ge 0 \text{ for all } x \in K\}.
\]
Describe the set \(K^*\).

Let v_1, \ldots, v_k \in \mathbf{R}^n, and let
\[
P = \operatorname{conv}\{v_1, \ldots, v_k\} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n.
\]
If f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} is convex, show that
\[
\sup_{x \in P} f(x) = \max_{i=1, \ldots, k} f(v_i).
\]

Let h : \mathbf{R}^k \to \mathbf{R} be a convex function with \operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}^k. For each i \in \{1, \dots, k\}, let g_i : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}, and define
\[
f(x) = h(g_1(x), g_2(x), \ldots, g_k(x)).
\]
Assume that for each i \in \{1, \dots, k\}, at least one of the following holds: (i) h is nondecreasing in its i-th argument, with all other arguments fixed, and g_i is convex; (ii) h is nonincreasing in its i-th argument, with all other arguments fixed, and g_i is concave; or (iii) g_i is affine. Show that f is convex on \mathbf{R}^n.

Let u \in \mathbf{R}^n and t \in \mathbf{R}_{++}, and define
\[
g(u,t)=t-\frac{1}{t}u^T u.
\]
Show that g is concave on the domain
\[
\{(u,t)\in \mathbf{R}^n\times \mathbf{R}\mid t>\|u\|_2\},
\]
using the convexity of the quadratic-over-linear function.

Let $u \in \mathbf{R}^n$ and $t \in \mathbf{R}$, and define
\[
g(u,t) = -\log\!\left(t - \frac{1}{t} u^T u\right).
\]
Let
\[
\operatorname{dom} f = \{(u,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid t > \|u\|_2\}.
\]
Using the result established previously, prove that $g$ is convex on $\operatorname{dom} f$.

Let
\[
f(x,t) = -\log(t^2 - x^T x), \qquad (x,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R},
\]
with domain
\[
\operatorname{dom} f = \{(x,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid t > \|x\|_2\}.
\]
Using the identity
\[
f(x,t) = -\log(t-\|x\|_2) - \log(t+\|x\|_2),
\]
prove that \(f\) is convex on \(\operatorname{dom} f\).

Let f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} be a convex function satisfying f(x) \ge 0 for all x \in \operatorname{dom} f, and let g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} be a concave function satisfying g(x) > 0 for all x \in \operatorname{dom} g. Define h on \operatorname{dom} h = \operatorname{dom} f \cap \operatorname{dom} g by
\[
h(x) = \frac{f(x)^2}{g(x)}.
\]
Show that h is convex.

Let f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} be a convex function with \operatorname{dom} f = \mathbf{R}^n and f(0) \leq 0. For fixed x \in \mathbf{R}^n, define g(x,t) = t f(x/t) for t > 0. Show that the function t \mapsto t f(x/t) is nonincreasing for t > 0.

Let $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be a convex function with $f(0) \le 0$, and let $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be a concave function such that $g(x) > 0$ for all $x \in \operatorname{dom} g$. Define
\[
h(x) = g(x) f\bigl(x / g(x)\bigr)
\]
on the domain
\[
\operatorname{dom} h = \{x \in \operatorname{dom} g \mid x / g(x) \in \operatorname{dom} f\}.
\]
Show that $h$ is convex on $\operatorname{dom} h$.

Define the function h : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R} by
\[
h(x) = \frac{x^T x}{\left(\prod_{k=1}^n x_k\right)^{1/n}},
\]
where
\[
\mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_k > 0,\ k = 1, \dots, n\}.
\]
Show that h is convex on \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.

Let $X \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n$ and $t \in \mathbf{R}_{++}$. Define
\[
f(X,t) = nt \log t - t \log \det X.
\]
Also define $g : \mathbf{S}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ by
\[
g(X) = n(\operatorname{tr} X) \log(\operatorname{tr} X) - (\operatorname{tr} X)(\log \det X).
\]
If $\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n$ are the eigenvalues of $X$, then
\[
g(X) = n\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\right)\left(\log \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i - \sum_{i=1}^n \log \lambda_i\right).
\]
Show that $f$ is convex in the pair $(X,t)$ on $\mathbf{S}_{++}^n \times \mathbf{R}_{++}$, and use this to prove that $g$ is convex on $\mathbf{S}_{++}^n$.

Let f : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R} be a convex function, and let A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}, b \in \mathbf{R}^m, c \in \mathbf{R}^n, and d \in \mathbf{R}. Define the domain of g by
\[
\operatorname{dom} g = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\},
\]
and define
\[
g(x) = (c^T x + d)\, f\!\left(\frac{Ax+b}{c^T x+d}\right), \qquad x \in \operatorname{dom} g,
\]
where \( (Ax+b)/(c^T x+d) \) denotes the vector \(Ax+b \in \mathbf{R}^m\) divided by the positive scalar \(c^T x+d\). Prove that g is convex on \(\operatorname{dom} g\).

Let a, c \in \mathbf{R}^n and b, d \in \mathbf{R}. Consider the function
\[
f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}, \qquad f(x) = \frac{a^T x + b}{c^T x + d},
\]
with domain
\[
\operatorname{dom} f = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid c^T x + d > 0\},
\]
and assume that \(\operatorname{dom} f\) is nonempty. Determine the conditions under which \(f\) is convex, and the conditions under which \(f\) is quasiconvex.

Let $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$ and $b \in \mathbf{R}^m$. Define $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ by
\[
f(x) = \frac{\|Ax-b\|_2^2}{1-x^T x},
\]
and let
\[
B = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid \|x\|_2 < 1\}.
\]
Show that $f$ is convex on $B$.

Let n \in \mathbf{N}, and let \alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n \in \mathbf{R} satisfy \alpha_k \ge 0 for k = 1, \ldots, n, and \sum_{k=1}^n \alpha_k \le 1. Define f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R} by
\[
f(x) = \prod_{k=1}^n x_k^{\alpha_k}.
\]
Prove that f is concave on \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.

Let f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} be a convex function, and define g by
\[
g(x,t) = f(x/t),
\]
with domain
\[
\operatorname{dom} g = \{(x,t) \in \mathbf{R}^n \times \mathbf{R} \mid x/t \in \operatorname{dom} f,\ t > 0\}.
\]
Prove that g is quasiconvex.

Let
\[
x=(x_1,x_2,x_3,x_4) \in \mathbf{R}^4,
\]
and define
\[
D=\left\{x \in \mathbf{R}^4 \mid x_4>0,\ x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}>0,\ x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}>0,\ x_1-\frac{1}{x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}}>0\right\}.
\]
Define the function $f:D \to \mathbf{R}$ by
\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{x_1-\frac{1}{x_2-\frac{1}{x_3-\frac{1}{x_4}}}}.
\]
Show that $f$ is convex on $D$ and decreasing in each variable on $D$.

Let n \in \mathbf{N} and x \in \mathbf{R}^n. Define the scalar Huber function f_{\mathrm{hub}} : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R} by
\[
f_{\mathrm{hub}}(t)=\begin{cases}(1/2)t^2 & |t| \le 1\\ |t|-1/2 & |t|>1.\end{cases}
\]
The circularly symmetric Huber function f_{\mathrm{cshub}} : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} is defined by
\[
f_{\mathrm{cshub}}(x)=f_{\mathrm{hub}}(\|x\|_2)=\begin{cases}(1/2)\|x\|_2^2 & \|x\|_2 \le 1\\ \|x\|_2-1/2 & \|x\|_2>1,\end{cases}
\]
where \|\cdot\|_2 denotes the Euclidean norm. Show that f_{\mathrm{cshub}} is convex, and find its conjugate function f_{\mathrm{cshub}}^*.

Let f : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\} be a convex function, and let x_1, \ldots, x_n \in \operatorname{dom} f. Suppose \lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n are scalars such that \lambda_1 > 0, \lambda_i \leq 0 for i = 2, \ldots, n, and \lambda_1 + \cdots + \lambda_n = 1. Show that
\[
f(\lambda_1 x_1 + \cdots + \lambda_n x_n) \geq \lambda_1 f(x_1) + \cdots + \lambda_n f(x_n).
\]

Let f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} be convex, and define
\[
g(x) = \inf_{\substack{z \in \mathbf{R}^n \\ z \ge 0}} f(x+z), \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n,
\]
where z \ge 0 is interpreted componentwise. Assume that g(x) > -\infty for all x \in \mathbf{R}^n. A function h : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} is called monotone nondecreasing if h(x) \ge h(y) whenever x \ge y componentwise. Show that g is convex, monotone nondecreasing, and satisfies g(x) \le f(x) for all x \in \mathbf{R}^n. Then, if h : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} is any convex monotone nondecreasing function such that h(x) \le f(x) for all x \in \mathbf{R}^n, prove that h(x) \le g(x) for all x \in \mathbf{R}^n. Conclude that g is the maximum convex monotone underestimator of f.

Let f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} be a convex function such that for every orthogonal matrix Q \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n} and every x \in \mathbf{R}^n, we have f(Qx) = f(x). Equivalently, f(x) depends only on \|x\|_2. Prove that there exists a function \phi : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R} with \operatorname{dom} \phi = \mathbf{R} such that \phi is convex and nondecreasing, and
\[
f(x) = \phi(\|x\|_2) \quad \text{for all } x \in \mathbf{R}^n.
\]
Also prove the converse: every function of the form f(x) = \phi(\|x\|_2), where \phi is convex and nondecreasing, is rotationally symmetric and convex.

Let $f_1, \ldots, f_m : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\}$ be convex functions. Define their infimal convolution $g = f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m$ by
\[
g(x) = \inf\{f_1(x_1) + \cdots + f_m(x_m) \mid x_1, \ldots, x_m \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ x_1 + \cdots + x_m = x\}, \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n.
\]
Show that $g$ is convex.

Let m \in \mathbf{N}, and for each i = 1, \ldots, m, let f_i : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{\infty\} be convex. Define their infimal convolution g = f_1 \diamond \cdots \diamond f_m by
$$g(x) = \inf\{f_1(x_1) + \cdots + f_m(x_m) \mid x_1, \ldots, x_m \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ x_1 + \cdots + x_m = x\}.$$ 
Let \operatorname{dom} g = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid g(x) < \infty\}. For each i = 1, \ldots, m, let the convex conjugate of f_i be
$$f_i^*(y) = \sup_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} (y^T x - f_i(x)),$$
and let the convex conjugate of g be
$$g^*(y) = \sup_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} (y^T x - g(x)).$$
Prove that
$$g^* = f_1^* + \cdots + f_m^*.$$

Let $A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}$ have rank $m$, let $f : \mathbf{R}^m \to \mathbf{R}$ be convex, and define $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ by $g(x) = f(Ax)$. Let
\[
f^*(u) = \sup_{z \in \mathbf{R}^m} (u^T z - f(z)), \qquad g^*(y) = \sup_{x \in \mathbf{R}^n} (y^T x - g(x)),
\]
and define $A^\dagger = (A A^T)^{-1} A$. Show that for every $y \in \mathbf{R}^n$,
\[
g^*(y) = f^*((A^\dagger)^T y),
\]
and that
\[
\operatorname{dom}(g^*) = A^T \operatorname{dom}(f^*).
\]

Let $n \ge 1$, and let $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n$ be positive real numbers. Define $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ by
\[
f(x) = \prod_{i=1}^n (1 - e^{-x_i})^{\lambda_i},
\]
with domain
\[
\operatorname{dom} f = \left\{x \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \mid \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i e^{-x_i} \le 1\right\}.
\]
For $i = 1, \ldots, n$, define
\[
y_i = \frac{\lambda_i e^{-x_i}}{1 - e^{-x_i}}, \qquad z_i = \frac{y_i}{1 - e^{-x_i}},
\]
and note that the Hessian is given by
\[
\nabla^2 f(x) = f(x)(yy^T - \operatorname{diag}(z)).
\]
Prove that $f$ is concave on $\operatorname{dom} f$.

Let $a,b \in \mathbf{R}$ with $a<b$. For each $x=(x_1,\dots,x_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n$, define the polynomial
\[
p_x(t)=x_1+x_2 t+x_3 t^2+\cdots+x_n t^{n-1}, \qquad t \in [a,b].
\]
Define $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ by
\[
f(x)=\sup_{t\in[a,b]} p_x(t)-\inf_{t\in[a,b]} p_x(t).
\]
Show that $f$ is convex on $\mathbf{R}^n$.

Let $f_i : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$, $i=1,\ldots,m$, be convex functions, and define
\[
\operatorname{dom} f = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid f_i(x) < 0,\ i=1,\ldots,m\}.
\]
For $x \in \operatorname{dom} f$, define
\[
f(x) = \sum_{i=1}^m e^{-1/f_i(x)}.
\]
Show that $f$ is convex on $\operatorname{dom} f$.

Let g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\} be a convex function, and let
\[
\operatorname{dom} g := \{ z \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid g(z) < +\infty \}.
\]
Suppose y \in \operatorname{dom} g. Define f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\} by
\[
f(x) = \inf_{\alpha > 0} \frac{g(y + \alpha x) - g(y)}{\alpha}, \qquad x \in \mathbf{R}^n,
\]
where the infimum is taken in the extended-real sense. Prove that f is convex.

Let $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be a convex symmetric function, meaning that $f(x) = f(Px)$ for every permutation matrix $P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$. Let $S \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$ be doubly stochastic, i.e., all entries of $S$ are nonnegative and each row sum and each column sum is equal to $1$. Show that for every $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, one has $f(Sx) \leq f(x)$.

Let $Y \in S^n$ have spectral decomposition
\[
Y = Q \, \operatorname{diag}(\lambda) \, Q^T,
\]
where $Q \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$ is orthogonal and $\lambda = (\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n$ is the vector of eigenvalues of $Y$. Define $S \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$ by $S_{ij} = Q_{ij}^2$ for all $i,j$. Show that $S$ is doubly stochastic and that
\[
\operatorname{diag}(Y) = S\lambda.
\]

Let $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be a convex symmetric function, meaning that $f(x) = f(Px)$ for every permutation matrix $P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n}$. Let $S^n$ denote the space of $n \times n$ real symmetric matrices. For $X \in S^n$, let $\lambda(X) = (\lambda_1(X), \ldots, \lambda_n(X)) \in \mathbf{R}^n$ be the vector of eigenvalues of $X$, and for any square matrix $A$, let $\operatorname{diag}(A) \in \mathbf{R}^n$ denote the vector of diagonal entries of $A$. If $\mathcal{V}$ is the set of $n \times n$ real orthogonal matrices, use the results of parts (a) and (b) to show that for every $X \in S^n$,
\[
f(\lambda(X)) = \sup_{V \in \mathcal{V}} f(\operatorname{diag}(V^T X V)).
\]
Deduce that the function $X \mapsto f(\lambda(X))$ is convex on $S^n$.

Let n \ge 1, and define the function f : \mathbf{R}^{n \times n} \times \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} by
\[
f(X,y) = y^T X^{-1} y,
\]
with domain
\[
\operatorname{dom} f = \{(X,y) \mid X + X^T \succ 0\}.
\]
Let S^n denote the set of n \times n real symmetric matrices. It is known that f is convex when restricted to X \in S^n. Determine whether f is convex on its full domain \operatorname{dom} f. If it is convex, prove it; if it is not, give a simple counterexample.

Let \operatorname{dom} g \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n be convex, and suppose that g is twice differentiable on \operatorname{dom} g. Define
\[
f(x) = -\exp(-g(x)), \qquad x \in \operatorname{dom} g.
\]
Assume that for every x \in \operatorname{dom} g,
\[
\begin{bmatrix} \nabla^2 g(x) & \nabla g(x) \\ \nabla g(x)^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \succeq 0.
\]
Show that f is convex on \operatorname{dom} g.

Let A \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times n}, b \in \mathbf{R}^m, and let \|\cdot\| be a norm on \mathbf{R}^m. Define f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} by
\[
f(x) = \max\{\|APx - b\| \mid P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times n} \text{ is a permutation matrix}\}.
\]
Show that f is convex.

Let g,h : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} be convex functions that are bounded below and satisfy \operatorname{dom} g = \operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}^n. Define f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} by
\[
f(x)=\inf\{\theta g(y)+(1-\theta)h(z) \mid y,z \in \mathbf{R}^n,\ \theta y+(1-\theta)z=x,\ 0 \le \theta \le 1\}.
\]
Show that f is convex, and describe \operatorname{epi} f in terms of \operatorname{epi} g and \operatorname{epi} h.

Let f : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R} \cup \{+\infty\}, and let \operatorname{dom} f = \{x \in \mathbf{R} \mid f(x) < +\infty\}. Prove that f is convex if and only if \operatorname{dom} f is convex and, for all x,y,z \in \operatorname{dom} f with x<y<z,
\[
\det
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 & 1 \\
x & y & z \\
f(x) & f(y) & f(z)
\end{bmatrix}
\geq 0.
\]

Let Y, Z \in \mathbf{S}^m satisfy \(0 \prec Y \preceq Z\). Show that
\[
\det Y \leq \det Z.
\]

Let X \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n be partitioned as
\[
X = \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} \\ X_{12}^T & X_{22} \end{bmatrix},
\]
where X_{11} \in \mathbf{S}^m, X_{12} \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times (n-m)}, and X_{22} \in \mathbf{S}^{n-m}. Consider the optimization problem
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
\text{minimize} & \log \det Y^{-1} \\
\text{subject to} & \begin{bmatrix} Y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \preceq \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} \\ X_{12}^T & X_{22} \end{bmatrix},
\end{array}
\]
with variable Y \in \mathbf{S}^m, where \log \det Y^{-1} has domain \mathbf{S}_{++}^m. Prove that the solution is
\[
Y = X_{11} - X_{12} X_{22}^{-1} X_{12}^T.
\]

Let $P \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times m}$ have rank $m$, and assume without loss of generality that
\[
P = \begin{bmatrix} I \\ 0 \end{bmatrix},
\]
where $I$ is the $m \times m$ identity matrix. For $X \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n$, partition $X$ conformally with $P$ as
\[
X = \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} \\ X_{12}^T & X_{22} \end{bmatrix},
\]
where $X_{11} \in \mathbf{S}^m$, $X_{22} \in \mathbf{S}^{n-m}$, and $X_{12} \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times (n-m)}$. Define
\[
f(X) = \log \det \left( X_{11} - X_{12} X_{22}^{-1} X_{12}^T \right)^{-1},
\]
with domain $\operatorname{dom} f = \mathbf{S}_{++}^n$. Using the result of part (b) and the minimization property from page 3-19 of the lecture notes, prove that $f$ is convex.

Let
\[
P = \begin{bmatrix} I \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbf{R}^{n \times m},
\]
where $I$ is the $m \times m$ identity matrix. Let $X \in \mathbf{S}_{++}^n$ be partitioned as
\[
X = \begin{bmatrix} X_{11} & X_{12} \\ X_{12}^T & X_{22} \end{bmatrix},
\]
where $X_{11} \in \mathbf{S}^m$, $X_{22} \in \mathbf{S}^{n-m}$, and $X_{12} \in \mathbf{R}^{m \times (n-m)}$. Prove that
\[
\left( X_{11} - X_{12} X_{22}^{-1} X_{12}^T \right)^{-1} = P^T X^{-1} P,
\]
and conclude that the function $f$ from part (c) can be written as
\[
f(X) = \log \det \left( P^T X^{-1} P \right).
\]

Let X be an \mathbf{R}^n-valued random variable with density p with respect to Lebesgue measure on \mathbf{R}^n, and assume that p is log-concave. Let g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} be a nonconstant affine function, and define Y = g(X). Determine whether Y must have a log-concave density with respect to Lebesgue measure on \mathbf{R}; either prove that this is true or give a counterexample.

Let $X$ be an $\mathbf{R}^n$-valued random variable with density $p : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}_+$ with respect to Lebesgue measure, and assume that $p$ is log-concave on $\mathbf{R}^n$. Let $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be a convex function, and define $Y = g(X)$. Determine whether the function $a \mapsto \mathbf{prob}(Y \leq a)$ is log-concave on $\mathbf{R}$. If it is, prove it; if not, give a counterexample.

Let X be a random variable on \mathbf{R}^n with a log-concave probability density with respect to Lebesgue measure, and let g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} be a measurable concave function. Define Y = g(X), and for s \in \mathbf{R} let (s)_+ = \max\{s,0\}. For a \in \mathbf{R}, define
\[
F(a) = \mathbf{E}\big((Y-a)_+\big),
\]
assuming this expectation exists for all relevant a. Determine whether F(a) is convex in a \in \mathbf{R}, and whether it is log-concave in a \in \mathbf{R}. Prove the statement or give a counterexample.

Let a \in \mathbf{R}^n, and let
\[
C = \{(a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n}) \mid (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n) \text{ is a permutation of } (1,2,\ldots,n)\} \subseteq \mathbf{R}^n.
\]
For y \in \mathbf{R}^n, define
\[
S_C(y) = \max_{x \in C} y^T x.
\]
If for any vector u \in \mathbf{R}^n, the components u_{[1]}, u_{[2]}, \ldots, u_{[n]} denote the entries of u arranged in nonincreasing order, show that
\[
S_C(y) = a_{[1]}y_{[1]} + a_{[2]}y_{[2]} + \cdots + a_{[n]}y_{[n]}.
\]

Let $a \in \mathbf{R}^n$, and let $C \subset \mathbf{R}^n$ be the set of all permutations of $a$, i.e.,
\[
C = \{(a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n}) \mid (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n) \text{ is a permutation of } (1,2,\ldots,n)\}.
\]
For $y \in \mathbf{R}^n$, define
\[
S_C(y) = \sup\{y^T z \mid z \in C\}.
\]
For any $u \in \mathbf{R}^n$, let $u_{[1]} \ge u_{[2]} \ge \cdots \ge u_{[n]}$ denote the components of $u$ sorted in nonincreasing order, and for $k = 1, \ldots, n$, define
\[
s_k(u) = u_{[1]} + u_{[2]} + \cdots + u_{[k]}.
\]
Given $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, prove that the condition
\[
x^T y \le S_C(y) \quad \text{for all } y \in \mathbf{R}^n
\]
holds if and only if
\[
s_k(x) \le s_k(a), \quad k = 1, \ldots, n-1, \qquad \text{and} \qquad s_n(x) = s_n(a).
\]
When these inequalities hold, one says that $a$ majorizes $x$.

Let $a \in \mathbf{R}^n$, and let
\[
C = \{(a_{\pi_1}, a_{\pi_2}, \ldots, a_{\pi_n}) \mid (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n) \text{ is a permutation of } (1, 2, \ldots, n)\} \subset \mathbf{R}^n.
\]
Let $S_C(y) = \sup_{z \in C} y^T z$ be the support function of $C$, and let its convex conjugate be
\[
S_C^*(x) = \sup_{y \in \mathbf{R}^n} (x^T y - S_C(y)).
\]
For $x \in \mathbf{R}^n$, say that $x$ is majorized by $a$ if, after rearranging the components of $x$ and $a$ in nonincreasing order, one has
\[
\sum_{i=1}^k x_{[i]} \le \sum_{i=1}^k a_{[i]} \quad \text{for } k = 1, \ldots, n-1,
\]
and
\[
\sum_{i=1}^n x_i = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i.
\]
Show that
\[
S_C^*(x) =
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } x \text{ is majorized by } a,\\
+\infty & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
\]
Conclude that $x$ is a convex combination of the permutations of $a$ if and only if $a$ majorizes $x$.

Let \(\theta \in \mathbf{R}^2\) satisfy \(\theta \succeq 0\) and \(\mathbf{1}^T\theta = 1\). Define \(f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^2 \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\[
f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} x_2^{\theta_2}.
\]
Show that \(f\) is concave on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^2\).

Let \(\theta = (\theta_1, \dots, \theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n\) satisfy \(\theta_i \ge 0\) for all \(i = 1, \dots, n\) and \(1^T\theta = 1\). Define \(f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}\) by
\[
f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} \cdots x_n^{\theta_n}, \qquad x = (x_1, \dots, x_n) \in \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.
\]
Show that \(f\) is concave on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\).

Let $n \geq 1$ and let $\theta = (\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n$ satisfy $\theta_i \geq 0$ for all $i$ and $1^T\theta \leq 1$, where $1 \in \mathbf{R}^n$ denotes the all-ones vector. Define $f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ by
\[
f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} \cdots x_n^{\theta_n}, \qquad \mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_i > 0,\ i=1,\ldots,n\}.
\]
Show that $f$ is concave on $\mathbf{R}_{++}^n$.

Let n \in \mathbf{N} and let \theta = (\theta_1, \dots, \theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n satisfy \theta_i \leq 0 for all i = 1, \dots, n. Define f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R} by
\[
f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} \cdots x_n^{\theta_n},
\]
where \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_i > 0,\ i = 1, \dots, n\}\). Prove that f is convex on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\).

Let n \geq 1 and let \theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n satisfy \mathbf{1}^T\theta = 1$. Define $f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ by
\[
f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} \cdots x_n^{\theta_n},
\]
where
\[
\mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_i > 0,\ i = 1, \ldots, n\}.
\]
Assume that exactly one component of \theta is positive; equivalently, there is exactly one index i such that \theta_i > 0, and \theta_j \leq 0 for all j \neq i. Prove that f is convex on \mathbf{R}_{++}^n.

Let n \in \mathbf{N} and let \theta = (\theta_1, \dots, \theta_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n. Consider the function f : \mathbf{R}_{++}^n \to \mathbf{R} defined by
\[
f(x) = x_1^{\theta_1} \cdots x_n^{\theta_n},
\]
where
\[
\mathbf{R}_{++}^n = \{x \in \mathbf{R}^n \mid x_i > 0,\ i = 1, \dots, n\}.
\]
Assume that \(1^T \theta \ge 1\), that there exists a unique index \(i\) such that \(\theta_i > 0\), and that \(\theta_j \le 0\) for all \(j \ne i\). Show that f is convex on \(\mathbf{R}_{++}^n\).

Let $f,g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be defined by
\[
f(x) = \|x\|_1, \qquad g(x) = \frac{1}{2}\|x\|_2^2,
\]
and let $h : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ be their infimal convolution,
\[
h(x) = \inf_{y \in \mathbf{R}^n} \left( \|y\|_1 + \frac{1}{2}\|x-y\|_2^2 \right).
\]
Define $\phi : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}$ by
\[
\phi(u) = \begin{cases} u^2/2, & |u| \leq 1,\\ |u|-1/2, & |u| > 1. \end{cases}
\]
Show that for every $x = (x_1, \dots, x_n) \in \mathbf{R}^n$,
\[
h(x) = \sum_{i=1}^n \phi(x_i).
\]

Let n \ge 1, and let h : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R} be a convex, nondecreasing function with \operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}, such that h(t) = h(0) for all t \le 0. Define f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} by
\[
f(x) = h(\|x\|_2).
\]
Show that f is convex on \mathbf{R}^n.

Let h : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R} be a convex, nondecreasing function with \operatorname{dom} h = \mathbf{R}, and suppose that h(t) = h(0) for all t \leq 0. Define f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R} by
\[
f(x) = h(\|x\|_2).
\]
For y \in \mathbf{R}^n, prove that
\[
f^*(y) = h^*(\|y\|_2).
\]

Let $p > 1$ and define $f : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ by
\[
f(x) = \frac{1}{p}\|x\|_2^p.
\]
Also define $h : \mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{R}$ by
\[
h(t) = \frac{1}{p}\max\{0,t\}^p = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{p} t^p & t \ge 0,\\ 0 & t < 0. \end{cases}
\]
Using the result from part (b), namely that if $g : \mathbf{R}^n \to \mathbf{R}$ is defined by $g(x) = h(\|x\|_2)$, then $g^*(y) = h^*(\|y\|_2)$ for all $y \in \mathbf{R}^n$, derive the convex conjugate $f^*$.
